target.name: Blackstar
target.founder: Daniel Edrisian (Founder/CEO, ex-OpenAI Codex engineer; previously founded Alex YC W24, an Xcode AI coding assistant acqui-hired by OpenAI Sept 2025; UC Berkeley EECS)

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $12M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in AI & Software (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 18 same-sector peers exist in AI & Software, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

data.narrative:
Blackstar is a Seed-stage AI & Software company founded by Daniel Edrisian, an ex-OpenAI Codex engineer whose prior venture (Alex, a YC W24 Xcode AI assistant) was acqui-hired by OpenAI in September 2025. The company has raised $12M to date. The relevant comp universe includes 9 private peers (Palantir, Waymo, Figure AI, Scale AI, Applied Intuition among them) and 1 public comparable (PLTR), though peers at identical stage and valuation tier are sparse.

Given data constraints, only the Stage-Progression method is applicable with sufficient rigor. Using total capital raised ($12M) as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 2.5x step-up multiplier from Seed to the subsequent stage yields $30M. A Peer-Median approach was not viable; while 18 same-sector peers exist in the AI & Software universe, fewer than three datapoints satisfied narrow filters for stage and tier alignment, making sector-wide median comparison unreliable and potentially misleading.

The triangulated valuation range is $26M (low) to $34M (high), with a median of $30M. This represents a 2.5x step-up from raised capital and reflects typical early-stage progression dynamics. Key uncertainties include the absence of tight peer comparables at Seed stage, the early revenue maturity typical of this cohort, and the founder's track record's precise valuation uplift relative to peer founders. The narrow method count (1) underscores the reliance on stage-based progression rather than market multiples.
